title: Regulatory Risk is Coming For AI | David Woo on AI Data Center CapEx and Iran War

segments.text: Today's episode is brought to you by the Tukurium Corn Fund ticker C O R N. Let's get into it.
Very pleased today to be joined by David Wu, independent economist, known for his game theory
based forecasts on global markets and geopolitics. David, welcome to monetary matters.
Thanks for having me. David, what do you think about AI, the huge
capex in data centers that's now global and just the AI data center trade?
For me, the big takeaway from the first quarter was the fact, by the way,
the combined capex of the five hybrid scalers that is Microsoft, Google, Amazon,
whatever, Oracle and Facebook were actually down for the first time in three years,
actually on a quarter on quarter comparison. In fact, even on your comparison, the growth rate dropped
relative to the fourth quarter last year. And then not to mention the fact that,
obviously, in the first quarter, these hyper-skillers were paying more for whatever they were
buying from Micron, Samsung and practically everything else. Okay, so as a result,
if they were paying more for the same stuff that they were buying and they're spending
actually less than they did in Q4, what this means is that actually capex in real terms
was probably down quite a bit. Okay, now it's interesting that why people got so excited because
well, say the earnings growth is going through the root of up 25%. But let me tell you why. Imagine
Microsoft, all Microsoft did was to pay $100 more per basically membership to Micron. Now Microsoft
only expense, let's just say 10% of that because they only expense the capex, you know, essentially
based on depreciation. So I just say they have to expense $10 of the $100 increase in Micron chip.
Okay, Micron, because this is just literally like free money because they just literally,
they don't even have to produce more, they're just charging more. That is 100% basically margin
business. Okay, so they get basically booked an increase in profit by $100. So 100 minus 10,
that is combined earnings go up by $90 just because Microsoft is paying $100 more to Micron.
Okay, for the same chip, what I'm saying to you is that ironically, the inflation of the components
that go into data centers have actually given ironically a boost to basically nominal earnings
growth because equity investors are dumb. They only look at nominal and rather than real for them.
Wow, nominal looks great. But I think so from now on, this is actually very important because so
I'm telling you, ironically, I don't disagree the notion that more capex to the extent that
hyper-skillers think that there is more upside for them in terms of productivity growth in terms of
whatever it is, that's bullish for AI. But I would argue by the way, not only there was a slowdown,
but actually the slowdown in real terms, poverty was quite considerable. On top of that in the first
quarter, I think the numbers were in general, okay, since should the window address by token maxing,
as you know, right? We know all these software engineers were racking up massive consumption
of AI because like, for example, Amazon, Amazon at a certain point were ranking their AI, whatever,
their software engineers based on who's using more AI, okay? So everybody was trying to max out,
you know, saying should the AI usage? As we saw, you saw the search in AI usage. This is why,
you know, what you probably heard this, you know, the CEO of Uber coming out a few weeks ago saying that
they ran through their entire token budget for the year in just four months and have very little
to show for and now they're rating back. So what I'm telling you is that very likely, we're probably
going to see a slowdown in Q2. So Q1 numbers were very impressive, no doubt about that, okay? And I
think it's for the wrong reason. I think Q2, you're probably going to see a slowdown. So you said
that there was a slowdown in actual cap X and Q1? Yes. Quarter over quarter. Wow. No one's
talking about that. In US dollar terms, there was a quarter and quarter drop. In a year and year,
basically growth rate decline in Q1. Wow. This is even before we take into account of the inflation,
probably we saw in Q1, which it would driven down, essentially, the real increase, the value
increase in the actual cap X. So what explains that quarter over quarter decrease in
CapEx from the fourth quarter of last year to the first quarter of this year and how come no one
else is talking about this? I think people just don't want to get a number. I mean, the main reason
is Microsoft actually Google actually increased their their their CapEx a little bit. Microsoft and
Facebook basically pull back a bit, which actually makes a lot of sense to me, actually because
Microsoft, we know that they have some issues, you know, because in the past, they thought that
they had this exclusive deal with Charlie PT with open AI, right? And then they lost that. And then
they've been trying to they are trying to and then they saw also the adoption, corporate adoption
of essentially Microsoft Copilot offering has been much basically worse than expected. This is

they've always felt that God, if I don't invest, I'm going to be left behind. Therefore, I'm just going

Microsoft historic has always been more conservative, but I think this is the reason why we saw a bit

the funny thing was that yeah, so Amazon and Google, they're like the CapEx basically picked up
again in Q1. But then again, both companies and basically book a massive evaluation of their

if you actually look at CapEx relative to operating earnings, for example, income, I mean,
there's no doubt. I mean, if you actually leave for the top for the five hyperskillers,

you know, I'm bearish, you know, I've been bullish oil and bearish equities and bearish gold. So
that's been sort of how I've been rowing for the last few months. And bearish gold as well.
Bearish going so far this short term. There's no doubt.
What do you think CapEx is going to be for this entire year? And what did you think of the CapEx
guidance that the companies gave in terms of how much money they expect to be spending over the course
of this fiscal year? I think many companies did increase their their CapEx guidance.
Again, there's a very big difference between increasing CapEx because you think that you're going

the timetable in terms of when the completion of these data center construction is going to be

you, I think, you know, we, we, otherwise, it's just basically income transfer. It's an income

we're going to build 10 million 10 homes and the cap we're guiding that that's going to cost us

that it's pretty, you know, like it just exaggerates the actually earnings growth momentum,
you understand? At an aggregate level for the entire index, that's what I'm saying to you.
Yes, yeah, I think you're absolutely right that the companies whose earnings are going up the most

And two, there's a lot more competition. You know, like there's only one company's basically

Samsung. So therefore, I can't even, I struggle to see how far you can see the kind of the pricing

the only reason why prices are going through the roof is because doing the pandemic, there was a

Okay. So like, but, but, but, but, but I think it goes way beyond that because I mean, there's,
